skill.name: agent-development
skill.description: Guides developers through creating Claude Code plugin agents with valid frontmatter, triggering examples, system prompt structure, tool selection, and validation steps.
skill.summary: Create and validate Claude Code plugin agents with clear triggers, prompts, tools, and examples.
skill.author: Dami-Randle

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found they are mostly markdown code fences, template text, ellipses, and substring matches in agent-development documentation. The only executable component is a local Bash validator that reads a user-supplied agent file with quoted arguments and does not execute file contents, contact networks, or exfiltrate data.

low_findings.title: Local Bash Validator Uses Shell Utilities
low_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE for script and local command usage, but low impact. The validator reads a user-provided agent markdown file with quoted paths and uses standard tools such as head, sed, awk, grep, and tail. I did not find evidence that it executes the file contents, writes files, contacts a network, or reads secrets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The shell commands and file reads are real, but their use is limited to local validation and quoted file arguments. No command execution of untrusted file contents or network behavior was found.

low_findings.title: Markdown Fences Flagged as Ruby Backtick Execution
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. Static detections in markdown files point to fenced examples, inline file names, and agent prompt templates. These are documentation examples, not Ruby backtick operators or executable code paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are markdown code fences or inline documentation. They are not executable Ruby code and do not create a command injection vector.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal Detections Are Template Ellipses
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. The path traversal alerts occur inside generated-agent examples and abbreviated prompt text. I did not find evidence of ../ path construction, unsafe path joining, or directory traversal logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are abbreviated examples using ellipses inside documentation. They do not form paths or interact with filesystem traversal logic.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto, SAM, and Reconnaissance Hits Are Substring Matches
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. The reported weak cryptography, Windows SAM database, system reconnaissance, and network reconnaissance hits appear in documentation text or variable names, not in cryptographic code, credential access, host enumeration, or network scanning logic. No evidence found for malicious intent.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is instructional text or benign variable usage. I found no cryptographic implementation, SAM database access, or reconnaissance commands at these locations.

content.value_statement: Developers need reliable agent files with correct triggers, tools, and system prompts. This skill provides templates, examples, and validation guidance for building Claude Code plugin agents.
actual_capabilities: Explains required agent frontmatter fields, including name, description, model, color, and tools.
Provides templates for agent descriptions with triggering examples and commentary blocks.
Shows system prompt structures for analysis, generation, validation, and orchestration agents.
Includes complete agent examples for review, testing, documentation, and security analysis workflows.
Provides a Bash validator for checking agent file structure and common quality requirements.
Guides tool selection with least-privilege examples for read-only, generation, and testing agents.
limitations: It targets Claude Code plugin agents and may not fit other agent formats without adaptation.
It provides templates and validation rules, not a full agent runtime or deployment system.
The validator checks structure and simple patterns, not semantic quality or trigger performance.
Generated agents still require human review for project-specific safety and correctness.

use_cases.title: Build a review agent
use_cases.description: Create a code review agent with clear triggers, scoped tools, and a structured review prompt.

use_cases.title: Standardize agent prompts
use_cases.description: Define repeatable system prompt patterns for teams that maintain multiple plugin agents.

use_cases.title: Validate contributed agents
use_cases.description: Check agent markdown files for required fields, naming rules, prompt length, and example blocks.

prompt_templates.title: Create a basic agent
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for one focused agent.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the agent-development skill to help me create a Claude Code agent for [task]. Recommend a name, trigger description, model, color, tools, and system prompt.

prompt_templates.title: Improve triggering examples
prompt_templates.scenario: Refine when an existing agent should activate.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this agent description and improve its triggering examples. Include explicit, implicit, proactive, and edge-case examples where appropriate.

prompt_templates.title: Design a specialized system prompt
prompt_templates.scenario: Create detailed behavior for an expert agent.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a structured system prompt for an agent that handles [domain]. Include responsibilities, process steps, quality standards, output format, and edge cases.

prompt_templates.title: Audit an agent set
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review across multiple plugin agents.
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate these agent files for naming, trigger specificity, tool permissions, prompt completeness, and overlap. Recommend concrete improvements for each agent.

output_examples.input: Create an agent that reviews TypeScript changes before commit.
output_examples.output: The skill would propose a focused reviewer agent with a valid name, blue or cyan color, read-only tools, proactive commit triggers, and a structured review prompt.

output_examples.input: Improve this agent description because it triggers too often.
output_examples.output: The skill would narrow the trigger conditions, add examples for when not to use the agent, and separate explicit requests from proactive activation cases.
The skill would recommend running the validator and reviewing any missing fields, overly broad tools, weak examples, or incomplete output format guidance.

best_practices: Use specific trigger conditions with two to four realistic examples.
Limit each agent to the smallest useful tool set for its task.
Define responsibilities, process steps, quality standards, output format, and edge cases.
anti_patterns: Do not use generic names such as helper, assistant, or agent.
Do not grant Bash or write access when a read-only agent is sufficient.
Do not rely on vague system prompts without concrete workflow steps.

faq.question: What does this skill help create?
faq.answer: It helps create Claude Code plugin agents with valid markdown structure, frontmatter, triggers, tools, and system prompts.

faq.question: Does it generate complete agents?
faq.answer: It provides templates and examples that guide agent creation. The final agent should still be reviewed and tested.

faq.question: Can it validate an agent file?
faq.answer: Yes. The included validator checks required fields, basic formats, prompt length, and description examples.

faq.question: Does it support Codex?
faq.answer: The marketplace metadata lists Codex support, but the guidance is mainly written for Claude Code plugin agents.

faq.question: How should I choose tools for an agent?
faq.answer: Start with the smallest tool set that can complete the task. Add write or shell access only when required.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. Review found documentation false positives and a low-risk local validation script, with no confirmed malicious behavior.
